value: gm-method lesson (2026-06-29): driving performance work across multiple independent repos. (1) STRUCTURE: one gm chain for bookkeeping + one subagent per repo, each working inside that repo, committing+pushing to that repo's own git -- repos are independent codebases so this parallelizes cleanly; each subagent told to measure-first, profile to locate the hot node, fix only what the profile justifies, re-measure for a before/after delta, and verify tests before pushing. (2) STALE-PRIOR DISCIPLINE: a pre-existing perf report/audit/baseline number is a CLAIM TO RETEST, not ground truth -- the conditions it was taken under may no longer hold or never matched reality (classic case: a headless software-raster FPS number that has nothing to do with real-GPU behavior). Re-measure the premise on the real surface before optimizing it. (3) HONEST NO-OP: when measurement REFUTES the premise (the indicted hot node is cheap, or the real cost lives in an out-of-scope dependency), the correct outcome is NO code change plus a documented correction -- inventing a change to show activity is the failure mode. An honest 'the thing I was sent to fix was never the problem' is a real result. (4) BYTE/BIT-IDENTICAL GUARD: for output-producing pipelines, verify the optimized output is identical (cmp/hash, golden-sample regression) so a speedup cannot silently change semantics. (5) ISSUES SURFACE DURING MEASUREMENT: a rotted perf-gate that silently stopped gating because an upstream check changed its output shape is the kind of real bug a measure-first pass catches -- fix it at root. (6) MEMORY HYGIENE: findings about a target repo (its paths, line numbers, perf numbers) belong in THAT repo's store, never the driver project's memory; the resolve auto-memo will leak them, so prune target-specifics from the driver's memory at session end and keep only the method lesson.
